rows.title: BC Information and Privacy Commissioner Reports 86% Rise in Complaints and AI-Generated File Surge in Legislative Address
rows.summary:
The Office of the Information and Privacy Commissioner for British Columbia, in a speech delivered by Commissioner Michael Harvey to the Select Standing Committee on Finance and Government Services, reported an 86 per cent increase in privacy and access complaints received in the past year, alongside a growing volume of files attributed to artificial intelligence large language models.

On 24 April 2026, Commissioner Michael Harvey addressed the Select Standing Committee on Finance and Government Services in Victoria, British Columbia, setting out operational updates for both the Office of the Information and Privacy Commissioner and the Office of the Registrar of Lobbyists. No financial penalties were announced in this address.

The Commissioner reported that the office received close to 3,900 files in the past year, representing an 86 per cent increase in privacy and access complaints, a 50 per cent increase in requests for review, and a 28 per cent increase in reported privacy breaches. The Commissioner notes that the total file count has more than doubled over the last five years, with the most recent year-over-year rise described as a sharp departure from the steady growth previously observed. The Commissioner states this increase has placed considerable strain on case review officers, investigators, adjudicators and management across the office.

The Commissioner identifies artificial intelligence large language models as one contributing factor to the surge in complaints. The Commissioner explains that individuals receiving Freedom of Information responses appear, in a number of instances, to be inputting those responses into large language models and using the outputs to generate complaints to the office. The Commissioner notes that while early resolution techniques have proven effective in closing many such files quickly, the known tendency of large language models to hallucinate, exaggerate and extrapolate means that other AI-generated files present greater complexity than standard matters. The Commissioner also reports that the office has introduced an early resolution investigator role, which has resolved approximately 104 additional files beyond expectations, and that the office is examining further procedural efficiencies including submission limits and restrictions on the number of active files a single individual may hold open at any one time. The Commissioner states that despite closing 36 per cent more files than in the previous year, a gap remains between file intake and resolution capacity that the office considers requires addressing in order to fulfil its legislative mandate.
rows.body_name: Office of the Information and Privacy Commissioner for BC
